name: One More Than Boarded
alias: the last passenger · the commuter the carriage counts twice, who alights where nothing stops
body: It is the sound that tells you first, or rather the absence of it: the last train at this hour is never silent, there is always the buzz of the tube lights and the long sigh of the doors, but on this train there is a stillness in one seat that the noise seems to bend around. You look up because the carriage felt fuller than it should at twenty-five past midnight, and you count the heads out of the same idle habit that makes you read every advertisement twice. You count them again. There is one more than boarded with you at the platform, and you did not see the doors open in between.

The last passenger does nothing that a tired commuter would not do. It sits with a bag on its lap and its face tilted toward the dark window, and it watches the tunnel as if there were scenery out there. The rule the tellers give is about the stops: you must not get off where it gets off. It rises a beat before the train slows, the way only someone who knows the line by heart can, and it leaves at a station whose name the recorded voice does not announce, a platform with the lights at half, tiled in a colour the network stopped using long ago. If your stop and its stop are the same, you stay seated. You ride one further into the night and you walk back. To follow it onto that platform is to be the one extra head on tomorrow's last train.

What stays with people is not fear exactly but a kind of courtesy, the way the thing keeps the manners of a passenger to the very end, even reaching up to steady itself on a handgrip the moment before the brakes catch. Those who have seen it say it always carries the same object, and here the detail is so domestic it unsettles the most: a single styrofoam packet of supper, the rubber band still on, gone cold, the gravy long since seeped through one corner, as though it had been bringing food home to someone for a very long time and had simply never arrived.
note: You hear this on the last services out of the interchanges, Jurong East, Bishan, the long run down the East West Line where the train surfaces and the windows turn to mirrors. SBS and SMRT night staff have their own quieter versions, mostly about the count on the platform CCTV never matching the count in the cabin. The student forums tell it as a warning dressed up as a dare, which is how the best of these survive.

The disputed point is always which closed station the passenger alights at; some name a halt that shut decades ago, others insist it is a platform that was sketched on an old map and never built at all, and no one agrees. The half-working ward is to keep your earphones in and your eyes on your own reflection, and above all not to count, because counting is the invitation. Probably nothing more than the brain inventing a stranger out of fatigue and an empty seat, and yet conductors still walk that final carriage twice.
